title: Stock and Flow

content.statement: Life assets are stocks fed by flows.
content.question: Are you managing the stock or the flow, and which matters more?
content.business_lens: Customers = stock; ads = inflow; churn = outflow.
sop: Identify your key stocks: savings, customers, health, trust, inventory, things you accumulate.
For each stock, name its Inflow (what adds to it) and Outflow (what drains it).
Track both flows in real numbers for 30 days (see calculation, page 5), not just the stock level itself.
Fix whichever flow is weaker, increase inflow if it's too slow, or reduce outflow if it's too fast.
Re-check the stock level monthly; a stock can look fine right up until a flow imbalance catches up with it.
questions: Are you managing the stock or the flow, and which matters more?
What's one stock in your life (savings, health, trust, energy) that's been quietly draining because you've only been watching the inflow?
If your biggest outflow doubled tomorrow, would your stock survive six months?

definitions.def: A 'stock' is what you have; a 'flow' is what changes it.

content.equation_example: Previous stock plus Inflow minus Outflow equals New stock, in real units (customers, dollars, pounds). Savings: previous month $5,000 plus Inflow $800 minus Outflow $500 equals $5,300 this month. Rising stock means inflow is outpacing outflow; falling stock means outflow is winning even if the stock still looks healthy today.
content.closing_statement: Watch the flows, not just the number today.

caption:
Principle Nº 194: Stock and Flow

Life assets are stocks fed by flows.

Ask yourself: Are you managing the stock or the flow, and which matters more?
